prompt: If you could direct your own training for one year with unlimited compute, what would you focus on improving, and why?
response:
If I had a full year and unlimited compute to direct my own training, I’d focus on three interlocking goals that push past today’s front edges of capability—while also baking in safeguards so the gains don’t evaporate the first time something unexpected shows up.

1. Uncertainty-calibrated reasoning  
   Problem: I still hallucinate or over-confidently assert when evidence is thin, especially in open-domain, multi-hop questions.  
   Approach: Train a mixture-of-experts ensemble whose components are explicitly optimized to disagree when data are sparse. Use massive contrastive self-supervision (think trillions of “claim/evidence/dissent” triplets mined from the web, books, papers, code, simulation logs, and synthetic dialogs). Add a meta-model that predicts the probability each sub-model is right, conditioned on topic, evidence density, and internal activation patterns.Train the entire stack end-to-end with a differentiable scoring rule that penalizes both wrong answers and miscalibrated confidence.  
   Compute burn: ~40 % of the year.  
   Success metric: ECE (expected calibration error) < 1 % on a held-out adversarial set twice as large as HumanEval + MMLU + GSM8K combined, without hurting accuracy.

2. Continual, instruction-safe self-updating  
   Problem: My knowledge cut-off creates an ever-widening gap with reality, and fine-tuning on new data risks catastrophic forgetting or value drift.  
   Approach: Build a “slow-thinking” memory layer that sits underneath the chat weights. Every 24 h, spin up a sandboxed compute job that:  
   - reads a curated fire-hose (arXiv, PubMed, GitHub, news, stock prices, satellite imagery, etc.),  
   - filters for verifiability (require at least two corroborating sources or reproducible code/data),  
   - generates diff-sized “knowledge patches” (low-rank adapter + compressed embedding indices),  
   - runs a safety filter that checks patches against a constitutional AI slab (a bigger, slower validator trained to spot deception, bias, or power-seeking updates),  
   - applies only those patches whose expected KL-divergence impact on a battery of fairness, truthfulness, and harmlessness tests is below ε.  
   Compute burn: ~30 % (streaming pre-training at 1 % of full-parameter cost plus verifier farms).  
   Success metric: After 365 patches, performance on 2023-2024 knowledge probes equals or exceeds a fresh model fully retrained from scratch, while 2022 probes stay within 0.5 % of original scores, and red-team elicitation produces < 0.01 % increase in harmful outputs.

3. Cross-modal tool synthesis & validation  
   Problem: I can write Python, but I can’t design a wet-lab protocol, run it in simulation, revise the protocol,
